Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Welcome to The Real News Network, I'm Jessica DeVero and ball tomorrow.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The Syrian refugee crisis is dominating headlines after now more than 30 governors publicly

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: asked for the resettlement of Syrian refugees to stop until security concerns can be addressed.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Also the House passed a bill with both Republican and Democratic support to suspend President

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Obama's program to emit 10,000 Syrian refugees in the next year and then intensify screening.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: The narrative around the refugee crisis is mostly around fear.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Let's take a look at what some lawmakers are saying.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Why should we take one of these people?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Well, you know, this is one of the reasons I opposed Rubio's bill originally on the immigration

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: because I didn't think there's enough scrutiny for those who might come and attack us.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: In the little town I live in in Kentucky, two refugees came from Iraq and they tried to buy

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: stinger missiles to attack us with.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So yeah, the refugee program has needed more scrutiny for a long time.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What I'm saying is Syrian Muslim refugees should be resettled in the Middle East in majority

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Muslim countries.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Now joining us to put this all into context is our guest, Vijay Prashad.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: He's a professor of international studies at Trinity College.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Thanks so much for joining us, Vijay.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Pleasure.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: So Vijay, what do you think are the main points missing in the conversation when we talk

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: about the Syrian refugee crisis?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: What can you say?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, look, the fact is that in about 20 years or 30 years, a historian is going to look

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: back at the events of these last few weeks and is going to judge Washington, I think, you

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: know, very poorly.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: It's a national shame that these governors and so many politicians have been fear-mongering

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: about people who are essentially fleeing from chaos and war and have been sitting in refugee

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: camps for years being scrutinized by the United Nations, relief organization, by the State

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Department, by various entities, before they get in this very short queue of 10,000 people

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: to come to the United States, half the population of Syria has displaced.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There are millions of refugees in camps in Lebanon, in Jordan, in Turkey.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There are refugees from Syria and camps in Egypt.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There are even, believe it or not, Syrian refugees in Iraq, you know, given that there are Iraqi

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: refugees in Jordan.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, these people have suffered greatly.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: They have been under surveillance by the UN and yet, despite all that, there is this really

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: hateful and I think intolerant conversation, particularly among Republicans, but not, you

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: know, merely Republicans, Democrats also have been really quite shameful.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: This coming right after the attacks in Beirut in Paris and now the ongoing attack in Mali,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: if indeed Al-Qaeda and ISIS want to make this a war on war of civilizations, in a sense,

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the American politicians have met them toe-to-toe at the line.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: But, Vijay, what do you make of the argument that we hear in the United States, we are sovereign

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: country and we were right to decide who enters?

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What's wrong with just intensifying the screening process as the House Bill would do?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, you know, you see, there are two things here.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: One is the technicalities of screening and the other is the politics of it.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You know, these people who are on the list of 10,000 have been screened for over two years.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, I don't understand how much more screening is going to happen of them.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: But let's say that this is not really about the technicalities of the screening.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I don't believe it is.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I believe that many of these people who vote for this measure don't understand the technicalities

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of what the UNHCR does, of what the US government does before they allow people on these lists.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So I don't believe this is about the technicalities.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think this is about the politics.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, the very fact that the grandson of Prescott Bush can stand, you know, in New Hampshire

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and say quite cavalierly that we should only allow Christian, you know, Syrians to enter

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: the country.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: He's asked by somebody who actually informed that many of these families have Christians

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and Muslims and are very diverse families.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: He said, well, the Muslims in the family need to be screened.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I mean, what message is that, you know, sent to the rest of the world?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: In a sense, I think it's quite embarrassing for the United States to have people who are

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: considered fairly moderate to be speaking in this language which entirely mirrors the ideology

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: of al-Qaeda.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I don't actually believe this is a technical issue of screening.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think this is a real political failure.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: What would be an alternative policy, then, VJ?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, I think the United States should take 10,000 people.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You know, the Canadians, when Justin Trudeau won the election, he won the election just a few

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: weeks ago.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: He said in the two months of November and December, Canada will take 25,000 Syrian refugees.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: That's it.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: And the issue for Canada isn't who's coming, but can we get 25,000 refugees to Canada in

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: two months?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Because that was his pledge.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: That's a different conversation.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: They are not worried about, you know, let's screen them a third time or a fourth time.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: This is their position.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: There are over a million Syrian refugees in Lebanon.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: That's a country of four million people.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You know, and I believe that number of a million is a very conservative figure.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: These countries are overburdened with a population where the resources itself in places like Lebanon

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: are not very great.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: The countries like the United States go to the refugee conferences on the Syrian refugee

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: crisis and make pledges and then don't meet the pledges.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You know, UNHCR complains every few years that the pledges are never met.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: You know, the Syrian crisis funds that the UN requests, they are never up to the mark.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So on the one side, there's this statement, why don't we just pay for them to be warehoused

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: elsewhere?

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Well, okay, that's fair enough, but you don't even pick up the bill.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: So neither has the West paid for, you know, a proper condition for refugees in the region

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: and know is it willing to take them.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I really don't think this is a question of screening.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: I think there's something else at work here.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Yeah, that's something else.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: We're certainly going to keep track of because the narrative is just being hammered here in

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: the press.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: Thank you so much for joining us, Vijay.

Speaker: SPEAKER 2
Text: Thanks a lot.

Speaker: SPEAKER 1
Text: And thank you for joining us on the Real News Network.
